title: Let there be information: rethinking cultural evolution
abstract: Prevailing accounts of cognitive evolution and cumulative culture frequently rely on narrative reconstructions and analogical projections, extrapolating from contemporary human cognition to infer the mental capacities of extinct humans. While such approaches may illuminate recent cultural developments, their applicability to deep prehistory is limited. We propose an alternative framework grounded in the thermodynamic imperative shared by several past and present members of the genus Homo: the reduction of entropy in organism–environment interactions. Central to this framework is the concept of Zones of Bounded Surprisal (ZBS), which formalises adaptive cognition in terms of the capacity to register, tolerate, and integrate new (surprising) information. Rather than treating cultural accumulation as having been continuous, uninterrupted and progressive, ZBS foregrounds information as a driver of evolutionary dynamics relevant in natural selection. We argue that stone-age vagaries of cultural evolution in Homo should be considered from the standpoint of the transmissibility of information between preliterate human beings — with variably evolving ZBS — under different demographic, social, and material constraints. This account foregrounds the role of ZBS in adaptive engagement with novelty and epistemic affordances by extant and extinct Homo.
